proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the perceived power and attractiveness of rural health tourism destinations, as expressed in online reviews, are not solely determined by service quality but are significantly mediated by platform-specific rating biases and the latent thematic interplay between 'natural/rural' and 'clinical/wellness' discourse.
proposals.method: We will collect a large corpus of reviews for rural health tourism destinations from major platforms. Using a BERT-based model (pre-trained, then fine-tuned for aspect-based sentiment analysis), we will extract perception themes and sentiment. We will statistically model the relationship between extracted themes (independent variables) and review ratings (dependent variable), controlling for reviewer factors inferred from metadata, to quantify the 'platform bias' effect. This analysis mirrors the causal inference approach used in Law's prior work on rating bias.
proposals.contribution: This study will contribute a scalable, computational framework for auditing the digital reputation of rural tourism. It will empirically identify the key narrative drivers of satisfaction in health tourism, moving beyond facility-level analysis to a systemic view of platform power. The findings will provide actionable insights for destination managers to craft more resilient online narratives.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in integrating NLP-derived thematic constructs with causal modeling of platform bias, a method applied to rating bias in other contexts but not yet to rural health tourism. It uniquely bridges the researcher's expertise in platform power, rural tourism, and experimental numerical analysis (e.g., GyroWEC performance) by applying a similarly rigorous, data-driven dissection to social text data.

proposals.title: Platform Power and Perceived Value: A Multi-Method Study of Consumer Perception Dynamics in a Rural Health Resort Micro-Destination
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 tourism research on online platforms and perception has focused on cities or national destinations, neglecting the unique dynamics of isolated, single-entity 'micro-destinations' like dedicated health resorts. These rural resorts represent a critical, high-value niche where platform ratings and reviews directly dictate survival, yet the interplay of service factors, consumer psychology (like variety-seeking), and platform power structures is poorly understood at this granular scale.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in a rural health resort micro-destination, the power of the online booking platform to shape consumer perception is disproportionately high, and that this influence is significantly mediated by specific on-site service factors and latent consumer behavioral traits, creating a distinct rating bias pattern compared to larger destinations.
proposals.method: We will apply an adapted perception study framework, integrating: 1) A quantitative analysis of the resort's online review data (e.g., from Booking.com, TripAdvisor) using factor analysis and structural equation modeling to identify key service factors and their relationship to ratings. 2) A controlled survey experiment measuring guests' variety-seeking behavior and its impact on post-consumption rating. 3) Numerical simulation techniques, inspired by the Gyroscopic Wave Energy Converter (GyroWEC) studies, to model the dynamic 'interference' and 'jitter' in perception signals caused by platform algorithms and external marketing shocks, adapting guidance system concepts from robotic fish tracking for stability analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, empirically-grounded model of perception formation for micro-destinations, bridging tourism studies with consumer behavior and control systems theory. It will offer resort managers targeted strategies to mitigate platform-dependent rating biases and provide platform designers insights into the unintended consequences of algorithmic curation in constrained choice environments.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the radical down-scaling of destination perception research to a single resort, treated as a closed system, and the interdisciplinary integration of tourism analytics with control theory metaphors (guidance, jitter elimination) and behavioral experiments to deconstruct platform power.

proposals.title: Platform Power and Perceptual Duality: A Gyroscopic Wave Energy Converter-Inspired Model to Contrast Domestic and International Tourist Perceptions in Rural China
proposals.motivation: China's tourism recovery post-2023 emphasizes both domestic demand and inbound revival, yet rural destinations face a critical challenge: digital promotion strategies optimized for domestic tourists may alienate international visitors due to cultural and platform biases. Understanding this perceptual duality is essential for equitable destination development and platform governance. This addresses a core tension in rural tourism between homogenizing platform power and the diverse needs of distinct tourist segments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant, modelable perceptual gap exists between domestic and international tourists regarding rural destination promotion, driven by algorithmic curation on dominant Chinese platforms and mediated by factors analogous to the gyroscopic stabilization of a wave energy converter under multi-axis excitation.
proposals.method: We adapt the Gyroscopic Wave Energy Converter (GyroWEC) numerical model as an analytical framework, treating domestic and international tourist cohorts as dual masses under roll (platform algorithmic bias) and pitch (cultural/demographic factors) excitations. The model's 'interference suppression' capability, akin to Rob's robotic fish guidance work, will be algorithmically implemented to isolate the core perceptual signal from platform noise. Data will be gathered via a controlled experiment on a simulated tourism platform, measuring engagement and rating bias, with variety-seeking behavior from Rob's prior work included as a key moderating variable in a structural equation model.
proposals.contribution: This study contributes a novel physics-inspired model to tourism perception analysis, providing a quantitative tool to diagnose perceptual gaps. It will offer empirical evidence on how platform power differentially shapes tourist segments, yielding practical insights for multi-audience content strategy. Theoretically, it bridges energy systems engineering with social science to explain stability and divergence in complex adaptive systems like tourism markets.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the transdisciplinary application of a GyroWEC's dynamic stabilization principles to model socio-technical perception gaps, and the explicit fusion of Rob Law's technical work on guidance systems and wave energy with his social science expertise on platform bias and rural tourism.

proposals.title: The Power of Platformed Presence: How Robotic Design Factors Mediate Tourist Emotional Response and Service Evaluation in Rural Tourism
proposals.motivation: The integration of service robots in tourism, particularly in rural areas where human resources are scarce, is accelerating. However, pre-2026 research lacks a nuanced understanding of how the design of these robotic platforms directly influences the core tourist experience. Understanding the emotional and evaluative impact of robot appearance and voice is critical for effective deployment, as negative reactions could undermine the service benefits and the destination's reputation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a congruence between robot design (appearance and voice) and the rural tourism context will elicit more positive emotional responses (e.g., pleasure, trust) and higher service evaluations, and that this relationship is mediated by perceived anthropomorphism and moderated by a tourist's variety-seeking behavior.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled, mixed-methods experiment. A physical robotic platform, adapted from a Gyroscopic Wave Energy Converter (GyroWEC) testbed for stable mobility, will be deployed in a simulated rural tourism info-point. Using a factorial design, we will manipulate robot appearance (mechanical vs. stylized-animal) and voice (synthetic vs. natural). Tourist participants will interact with the robot. Emotional response will be measured via facial expression analysis (using pre-2026 Affectiva or OpenFace APIs) and self-report (PANAS). Service evaluations and perceived anthropomorphism will be captured via survey. We will analyze the data using structural equation modeling (SEM) to test the mediation and moderation effects, examining the factors between design and outcome.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a validated framework linking specific robotic design elements to quantifiable emotional and service outcomes in tourism. It will provide empirical evidence for platform developers and tourism operators on optimal design choices for rural settings. Furthermore, it will extend theories of human-robot interaction into the under-studied domain of experiential tourism.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in synthesizing Rob Law's expertise in tourism platform power dynamics and consumer variety-seeking with human-robot interaction design, and grounding the experiment in a tangible, mobility-capable platform derived from applied engineering research (GyroWEC), moving beyond purely digital or static robot studies.

proposals.title: Biometric Feedback Loops for Adaptive Service Robots: Measuring Tourist Stress and Engagement in Rural Platform Economies
proposals.motivation: The deployment of service robots in rural tourism, a growing pre-2026 trend, creates new power dynamics between tourists, platforms, and automated services. Current evaluations rely on subjective ratings, which are prone to biases like variety-seeking. There is a critical gap in understanding the real-time, physiological impact of human-robot interaction (HRI) in these unstructured, often stressful rural tourism contexts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a closed-loop control system for a service robot, using real-time biometric feedback (EEG-based cognitive load and eye-tracking-based visual engagement), will significantly reduce tourist stress and increase engagement compared to robots operating on pre-programmed or simple reactive protocols.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled field experiment in a rural tourism setting. Participants will interact with a robotic guide/fish. We will collect synchronized EEG (using a wearable headset like Emotiv EPOC+), eye-tracking (Pupil Labs Core), and robot sensor data. The robot's 'Interference Suppression and Jitter Elimination' tracking algorithm will be adapted to incorporate a physiological state estimator. A factor analysis will model the relationship between biometric factors (stress/engagement), environmental factors (e.g., crowd density), and robot service parameters, creating a multi-input adaptive controller.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for quantifying HRI quality through objective physiology, moving beyond post-hoc surveys. It will produce a validated adaptive control architecture for tourism robots that balances task efficiency with user wellbeing. The findings will inform the design of equitable platform economies by revealing hidden stressors in automated rural services.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in fusing HCI physiological sensing methods with robust robotic guidance control, specifically for the under-studied context of rural tourism, and using this fusion to directly address power imbalances in service platforms through real-time biomechanical adaptation.

proposals.title: Discursive Power and Service Frames: A Sociolinguistic Analysis of Human-Robot Interaction in Rural Tourism Platforms
proposals.motivation: The deployment of service robots in rural tourism, a sector often characterized by limited resources and complex power dynamics between hosts and guests, presents a critical challenge in assessing service quality. Pre-2026 research on robotic service evaluation relies heavily on numerical ratings and surveys, which fail to capture the nuanced, interactional construction of satisfaction or frustration. A detailed analysis of the conversation itself is needed to understand how language use shapes the tourist experience and reveals underlying biases in automated service platforms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systematic discursive analysis of tourist-robot dialogues will reveal distinct conversational frames and power asymmetries that correlate strongly with, and provide deeper explanatory power for, traditional numerical service quality metrics. Specifically, we posit that robots failing to manage conversational repair or topic alignment will trigger discursive patterns associated with negative evaluations.
proposals.method: We will collect audio/video logs from robotic concierges deployed in a rural tourism case study. Applying Interactional Sociolinguistics and Conversation Analysis, we will code transcripts for discursive features: turn-taking patterns, adjacency pairs (e.g., question-answer), repair sequences, and framing moves. This qualitative analysis will be integrated with pre-2026 computational methods: we will use Natural Language Processing (NLP) architectures like BERT for sentiment and topic modeling, and apply gyroscopic wave energy converter (GyroWEC) analysis techniques—specifically, methods for isolating signal (cooperative discourse) from interference (miscommunication) and jitter (conversational dysfluencies)—to quantify discursive stability and its relationship to user-reported satisfaction factors.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, discourse-centered framework for evaluating service robot effectiveness, moving beyond rating aggregates to the mechanics of interaction. It will provide tourism platform designers with specific linguistic targets for improving robotic agent scripts. Furthermore, it will demonstrate the utility of sociolinguistic theory in human-robot interaction (HRI) and show how signal-processing metaphors from engineering (e.g., GyroWEC) can inform the analysis of conversational data.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the interdisciplinary fusion of rigorous sociolinguistic discourse analysis with Rob Law's established technical work on platform power dynamics and GyroWEC performance under perturbation, applying these to the under-studied context of rural tourism HRI. This approach treats conversation as a measurable system with stability parameters, a non-obvious synthesis given existing, siloed research in HRI, tourism, and sociolinguistics.

proposals.title: Balancing Risk and Return: A Strategic Portfolio Analysis of Robotic Service Mix in Rural Tourism Platforms
proposals.motivation: The adoption of service robots in hotels, particularly in rural tourism, is accelerating but remains a high-risk, capital-intensive innovation. Pre-2026, hoteliers face significant uncertainty in selecting and integrating robots (e.g., concierge, cleaning, delivery) without a strategic framework to manage the portfolio of robotic assets. This research addresses a critical gap in understanding how to optimize a hotel's robotic service mix to balance financial returns, operational reliability, and guest experience risks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hotel's mix of robotic services can be modeled as an innovation portfolio, where an optimal balance between high-risk/high-return (e.g., interactive social robots) and low-risk/core-function (e.g., automated vacuuming) robotic investments leads to superior overall service platform performance and resilience.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Boston Consulting Group (BCG) Growth-Share Matrix and real options analysis to classify robotic assets. Data will be collected via a survey of rural hotels using robotic platforms, measuring factors like cost, adoption rate, failure frequency, and guest rating impact. A numerical model, inspired by the Gyroscopic Wave Energy Converter (GyroWEC) performance studies, will simulate portfolio 'jitter' (operational instability) and 'interference' (task conflict) under varying demand conditions. Regression analysis will test the relationship between portfolio balance metrics and key performance outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel strategic management tool for technology investment in hospitality, moving beyond single-robot evaluations. It will contribute to the literature on tourism platform power dynamics by showing how resource allocation in robotic assets shapes competitive advantage. The study will also offer empirical evidence on managing systemic risk in automated service operations.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying financial and strategic portfolio theory to the physical asset mix of service robots, a domain previously dominated by technical feasibility or single-case studies. Integrating concepts from wave energy converter stability analysis ('jitter elimination') into a service management context for risk assessment is also a unique methodological crossover.

proposals.title: The Power of a Single Query: How Micro-Interactions with Robot Concierges Influence Guest Satisfaction in Rural Tourism Platforms
proposals.motivation: The adoption of service robots in tourism, particularly in rural settings with limited staff, is accelerating. While platforms aggregate guest ratings, the power dynamics and satisfaction drivers of brief, automated service encounters are poorly understood. Prior work on online rating bias, like variety-seeking behavior, suggests these micro-interactions could disproportionately impact overall evaluations, yet this remains unstudied in human-robot service contexts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a single, successful robot-concierge interaction for a specific guest query will have a non-linear, outsized positive effect on overall guest satisfaction scores, mediated by the perceived power and reliability of the service platform. Conversely, a failed micro-interaction will trigger a significant negative rating bias.
proposals.method: We will deploy a prototype robotic fish platform, adapted with a concierge interface, in a controlled rural tourism setting (e.g., a lodge). Building on our prior work, we will use the Interference Suppression and Jitter Elimination Ability-Based Adaption Tracking Guidance algorithm to ensure smooth, natural robot movements during interactions. We will experimentally manipulate the success/failure of a single information query (e.g., dinner reservation) and measure subsequent overall satisfaction. Factors such as user expectations and platform trust will be analyzed as mediators using structural equation modeling, linking the robotic service encounter to platform-level outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This study will contribute a fine-grained, causal understanding of how atomic robotic service events influence macro-level platform metrics like ratings. It will extend theories of service quality and rating bias into the realm of human-robot interaction. Practically, it will offer design principles for optimizing brief robotic interactions to maximize guest satisfaction and platform reputation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in scaling down the analysis unit to a single robot-led query and linking it to platform power dynamics, a gap between micro-HRI studies and macro-platform literature. Methodologically, it applies guidance algorithms from robotic fish control to stabilize service interactions, a crossover from engineering to tourism studies.

proposals.title: Operationalizing Uncertainty: A Framework for Cost-Benefit Analysis of AI Hallucination Management in Rural Tourism Platforms
proposals.motivation: The integration of generative AI into travel and tourism platforms, particularly for rural areas with sparse and dynamic information, is accelerating pre-2026. Current platform design and service models often assume AI outputs are reliable, creating significant operational risks. A critical, unaddressed gap exists in quantifying the costs and operational burdens of detecting and mitigating AI hallucinations (e.g., incorrect transport schedules, non-existent amenities) in these critical, resource-constrained service contexts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the operational cost of managing AI hallucinations in a rural tourism platform scales non-linearly with service complexity and that implementing a hybrid human-AI verification protocol, informed by a dynamic risk model, will yield a superior cost-benefit ratio compared to purely automated or human-only moderation.
proposals.method: We will develop a simulated rural tourism service platform environment. Using a fine-tuned GPT-3.5/4 or open-source LLM (e.g., Llama 2), we will generate itinerary and Q&A outputs, intentionally seeding hallucinations based on pre-2026 failure mode analysis. We will model 'management' costs using an adaptation of the Gyroscopic Wave Energy Converter (GyroWEC) control logic—treating hallucination detection as a stabilization problem—to dynamically allocate verification resources ('factors') between AI self-check modules and human-in-the-loop tiers ('service'). The core algorithm will be a cost-optimization model weighing risk, response time, and resource expenditure.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative framework for assessing the true operational overhead of generative AI in critical tourism services. It will contribute a novel, adaptable risk-management architecture for platform designers, moving beyond accuracy metrics to total cost of ownership. The findings will directly inform the sustainable deployment of AI in rural and resource-limited tourism ecosystems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying control theory concepts from wave energy (GyroWEC) to model AI uncertainty management as a dynamic stabilization task, and in shifting the evaluation focus from pure output quality to the systemic operational costs and power dynamics between automated systems and human oversight in platform governance.

proposals.title: Power Dynamics and Algorithmic Bias: A Governance Framework for AI-Driven Rural Tourism Platforms
proposals.motivation: The rapid deployment of AI in tourism platforms, from dynamic pricing to personalized recommendations, introduces significant risks of bias, privacy erosion, and opaque accountability, particularly in rural contexts where platform power is concentrated. Pre-2026 research, including studies on online rating bias and service factors, highlights these vulnerabilities but lacks integrated, actionable governance models. This creates an urgent need for ethical frameworks that protect rural communities and tourists while ensuring sustainable platform development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-stakeholder governance framework, co-created with rural communities and incorporating algorithmic audits for bias detection, will significantly improve perceived fairness, privacy assurance, and accountability in AI-driven tourism platforms compared to current industry self-regulation.
proposals.method: The research will employ a mixed-methods approach. First, we will conduct a systematic review of AI ethics guidelines and existing platform governance. Second, using agent-based modeling (inspired by methods for analyzing complex systems like gyroscopic wave energy converters), we will simulate the interaction between platform algorithms, user variety-seeking behavior, and rural service providers to identify bias propagation pathways. Third, we will develop and test a prototype framework using techniques like fairness-aware machine learning (e.g., adversarial debiasing) and differential privacy, validated through stakeholder workshops in selected rural tourism case studies.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, empirically-grounded governance framework specifically tailored for AI in rural tourism, moving beyond high-level principles. It will provide a methodological toolkit for auditing algorithmic bias in recommendation and pricing systems, directly addressing the 'hidden drivers' of platform inequality. The research will advance the interdisciplinary study of power, technology, and rural development.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in synthesizing Law's expertise in rural tourism, platform power, and complex system analysis (e.g., GyroWEC studies) to tackle AI ethics, a domain not directly addressed in his prior work. It proposes applying system dynamics and simulation modeling from engineering to the socio-technical problem of governance, creating a concrete bridge between ethical theory and platform engineering.

proposals.title: Power Dynamics in the Platform: A Study of Generative AI's Influence on Narrative Personalization and Perceived Authority at Rural Heritage Sites
proposals.motivation: The integration of generative AI into cultural heritage interpretation, particularly for rural tourism sites, presents a critical shift in how narratives are created and consumed. While offering potential for personalized engagement, this AI-driven platformization risks centralizing narrative authority, potentially marginalizing local community voices and flattening complex histories. Understanding the interplay between AI-generated personalization and the power dynamics shaping tourist experience is an urgent pre-2026 research gap at the intersection of tourism studies and digital platform governance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the degree of personalization offered by a generative AI heritage interpreter significantly increases user satisfaction but simultaneously reduces the perceived authority and authenticity of the narrative, with this effect being more pronounced for visitors with high variety-seeking behavior.
proposals.method: We will develop a prototype AI interpretation platform using a fine-tuned GPT-3 (or similar large language model) architecture, trained on verified historical data and local community-sourced narratives for a specific rural heritage site. An experimental study will expose participant groups to different interpretation conditions (static, AI-personalized). We will adapt methods from Law's prior work, using gyroscopic sensor data (like that from Gyroscopic Wave Energy Converter studies) to capture subtle, non-conscious visitor engagement and jitter (analogous to robotic fish tracking) as behavioral metrics, triangulated with surveys measuring satisfaction, perceived authority, and variety-seeking (extending his online rating bias research).
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for evaluating AI in heritage tourism that moves beyond usability to critically assess narrative power and control. It will provide empirical evidence on how algorithmic personalization interacts with human factors like variety-seeking to shape heritage perception, offering practical insights for ethical AI deployment in rural and cultural tourism platforms.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in synthesizing Law's expertise in physical system dynamics (gyroscopic sensing) and behavioral factors (variety-seeking) to measure the implicit, non-conscious impacts of AI narrative generation, a method not previously applied to critique the power structures within digital heritage platforms.

proposals.title: Platform Power and Rural Resilience: A Multi-Agent AI System for Real-Time Crowd Flow Management at Mega-Tourism Events
proposals.motivation: Mega-events in rural or peri-urban areas, such as music festivals or cultural gatherings, create acute management challenges, straining local infrastructure and services. Pre-2026, digital platforms shape tourist behavior, but their top-down design often fails to account for real-time, physical crowd dynamics and local power imbalances, leading to safety risks and negative community impacts. This research addresses the critical gap between online platform governance and on-the-ground crowd control.
proposals.hypothesis: A decentralized, multi-agent AI system, integrating real-time sensor data with predictive models of variety-seeking behavior, can optimize crowd flow and information dissemination more effectively than centralized platforms, thereby enhancing safety and distributing managerial 'power' across the event ecosystem.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid simulation-testbed. First, a numerical study using agent-based modeling (ABM) will simulate crowd movements, incorporating factors like service point locations and 'variety-seeking' behavioral parameters derived from online rating data. Second, this digital model will be integrated with a physical testbed using robotic platforms (adapted from robotic fish guidance algorithms) to emulate autonomous, mobile information nodes. The core AI will use multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) for decentralized decision-making, with system stability analyzed using methods inspired by Gyroscopic Wave Energy Converter (GyroWEC) dynamics under perturbing 'exciting motions' analogous to crowd surges.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for 'adaptive tracking guidance' in human crowds, bridging tourism studies, robotics, and complex systems engineering. It will produce actionable insights into how AI can mediate between platform-driven tourist behavior and physical infrastructure constraints, offering a blueprint for more resilient rural event management.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in synthesizing Law's expertise on platform power and variety-seeking with control theory from marine robotics and gyroscopic stability analysis to create a bio-inspired, decentralized management paradigm, moving beyond conventional centralized surveillance or static scheduling approaches.

proposals.title: The Dual Role of AI in Destination Crisis Communication: A Platform-Power Analysis of Disinformation Generation and Detection in Rural Tourism
proposals.motivation: Tourism destinations, especially rural ones, are acutely vulnerable to reputational crises, where online misinformation can rapidly deter visitors and devastate local economies. Pre-2026, AI's capacity for generating convincing synthetic text and media (e.g., via GPT-3, DALL-E) presents a novel threat for malicious disinformation campaigns. Concurrently, AI-powered content moderation and fact-checking tools are being deployed by platforms, creating a critical power dynamic between generative and detective AI systems that remains unexplored in tourism crisis contexts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in a tourism crisis, AI-generated disinformation will exploit specific cognitive and platform-based factors (e.g., variety-seeking in information consumption) to amplify bias and jitter in online ratings and narratives, but that an adaptive AI detection framework, modeled on control system principles, can effectively suppress this interference.
proposals.method: The study employs a dual-method experimental and numerical simulation approach. First, we will use a fine-tuned large language model (e.g., GPT-3) to generate crisis-specific disinformation narratives, testing their persuasiveness via online experiments that measure changes in destination perception and rating behavior. Second, we will develop a detection model using a BiLSTM with attention mechanisms and graph neural networks to identify AI-generated text and coordinated inauthentic behavior on platforms. This detection system will be designed with an adaptive feedback loop, inspired by robotic guidance control from Law's prior work, to dynamically adjust to evolving disinformation tactics.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a novel, empirically tested framework for understanding the AI-driven disinformation lifecycle in tourism crises. It will produce a new dataset of AI-generated tourism crisis narratives and offer a proof-of-concept adaptive detection tool. The findings will inform both platform governance and destination management organization crisis communication strategies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in synthesizing Law's expertise in platform power dynamics, consumer behavior (variety-seeking), and control system engineering (guidance, jitter elimination) to model the adversarial interplay between generative and detective AI—a 'dual problem' previously studied in isolation. Applying a gyroscopic 'stabilization' analogy to information ecosystems in rural tourism is a unique interdisciplinary leap.

proposals.title: The Rural Tourism Lens: Quantifying Geographic and Cultural Bias in Foundational LLMs from Platform Data
proposals.motivation: Foundational Large Language Models (LLMs) are trained on vast, uncurated web corpora, which are heavily shaped by platform-mediated content like tourism reviews and itineraries. This creates a critical inverse problem: the data's inherent biases—towards popular, well-reviewed, and easily accessible destinations—are encoded into the model's world knowledge, potentially marginalizing rural and less-documented locales. Understanding this data-to-model influence is essential for assessing the fairness and representational power of AI in applications like recommendation systems and policy planning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the geographic and thematic distribution of tourism data in pre-training corpora (e.g., Common Crawl) introduces a measurable, recency- and popularity-skewed bias in LLMs, systematically disadvantaging representations of rural tourism and associated cultural narratives compared to major urban centers.
proposals.method: We will construct a stratified dataset from pre-2026 sources, contrasting high-volume urban tourism data (e.g., TripAdvisor reviews for global capitals) with lower-volume rural tourism data (e.g., niche blog itineraries, local guide sites). Using probing techniques and representation similarity analysis, we will quantify bias in a pre-trained model like GPT-2 or BERT. We will then fine-tune a controlled model (e.g., a T5 variant) on curated subsets, applying causal mediation analysis to isolate the influence of tourism data on model outputs. The gyroscopic wave energy converter (GyroWEC) study's methods for analyzing system performance under specific exciting motions (roll/pitch) inspire our approach to testing model stability under controlled 'data excitation' inputs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for auditing the provenance of geographic and cultural bias in LLMs, linking it directly to a specific data domain (tourism). It will contribute to the field of AI ethics by operationalizing the study of data influence, and to tourism studies by revealing how platform data dynamics may be fossilized in generative AI, affecting the perceived value of destinations.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines bias in LLMs or bias in online ratings, this proposal uniquely inverts the causal direction to treat the LLM as an output to be reverse-engineered, explicitly tracing how tourism platform data—a key source of geographic and experiential narrative—shapes foundational model knowledge. It merges Law's expertise in tourism platforms and power with a novel, data-influence-focused AI audit methodology.

proposals.title: Power and Place in Platformization: Developing and Testing Regional AI Models for Rural Tourism Service Ecosystems
proposals.motivation: The dominance of global, one-size-fits-all AI models in tourism platforms risks eroding local cultural nuance and economic agency, particularly in rural areas. These models, trained on generic data, often fail to understand regional dialects, specific place-based knowledge, and non-standard service factors, leading to biased recommendations and a power imbalance that favors standardized, platform-friendly offerings over authentic local experiences. This research addresses the need for AI systems that respect and amplify local context within the digital service economy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that small, localized language models (SLMs) fine-tuned on region-specific tourism data (e.g., local reviews, dialectal text, and hyper-local attraction descriptions) will outperform global large language models (LLMs) in generating accurate, context-aware, and culturally relevant recommendations for rural tourism, thereby redistributing informational power within the platform ecosystem.
proposals.method: We will adopt a comparative, factor-based experimental design. First, we will curate a novel dataset of online reviews, local guide texts, and social media content from a specific rural tourism region. We will then fine-tune a pre-trained, compact transformer architecture (e.g., a distilled version of BERT or GPT-2, as available pre-2026) on this corpus. The performance of this regional SLM will be rigorously tested against a baseline general-purpose LLM API (e.g., GPT-3.5) under controlled conditions, evaluating metrics like recommendation relevance, dialect comprehension, and cultural accuracy. The experimental framework will be informed by methods from the researcher's prior work on system performance under variable conditions (Gyroscopic WEC) and bias analysis (online rating studies).
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel methodological framework for developing and benchmarking place-based AI in tourism. It will provide empirical evidence on the trade-offs between scale and specificity in AI for service platforms, and offer a concrete technical pathway for communities to assert greater control over their digital tourism representation. The findings will advance the discourse on decentralized AI and its role in sustainable rural development.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in synthesizing the concept of 'scale variance' from spatial analysis with SLM development to address power dynamics in tourism platformization, a direct application not yet explored in pre-2026 literature. It moves beyond identifying platform biases (like variety-seeking) to actively engineering a counter-model grounded in local data sovereignty.

proposals.title: Platform Power and Ethical Inversion: A Study of Tourist and Employee Agency in Rural Tourism Technology Governance
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on platform ethics in tourism predominantly focuses on corporate management as the primary ethical actor, framing tourists and employees as passive subjects. This overlooks their potential as active agents who can subvert, adapt, or resist platform-imposed ethical frameworks. In rural contexts, where platform power dynamics are pronounced and community impacts are acute, understanding this agency is critical for designing equitable and sustainable digital tourism ecosystems.
proposals.hypothesis: Tourists and platform-mediated service employees in rural tourism are not merely ethical subjects but are key agents whose behaviors and decisions significantly mediate the ethical outcomes of technology use, often in ways that invert or circumvent top-down managerial ethical directives.
proposals.method: We will employ a mixed-methods, multi-actor study. First, we will analyze online rating data from a rural tourism platform using an adapted causal inference model (e.g., a Doubly Robust Estimator) to isolate the 'variety-seeking' behavioral factor identified in Law's prior work as a driver of bias, testing its interaction with platform nudges. Second, we will conduct in-situ ethnographic observations and interviews with tourists and homestay employees, using a 'gyroscopic' analytical framework inspired by Law's WEC studies to model how actors maintain ethical equilibrium under the roll (tourist demands) and pitch (platform rules) of conflicting pressures.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a novel, agent-centric model of ethical technology governance in tourism, shifting the scholarly conversation from corporate responsibility to distributed agency. It will provide empirical evidence on how micro-behaviors of users and workers shape macro-ethical outcomes, offering practical insights for platform design and local policy that supports ethical resilience in rural communities.
proposals.novelty: The study inverts the dominant ethical actor framework in tourism technology studies and uniquely synthesizes concepts from wave energy converter dynamics (gyroscopic stability under perturbation) with social science analysis of platform-mediated behavior to model ethical agency.

proposals.title: Pluralistic Ethics in Platform Governance: A Multi-Framework Analysis of Rural Tourism Technology Deployment
proposals.motivation: The global expansion of tourism platforms and technologies, such as wave energy converters for coastal resorts, often assumes a single, universal ethical framework (e.g., Western-centric utilitarianism) for managing data, labor, and environmental impact. This is problematic as it can marginalize rural and indigenous communities whose values around land, data sovereignty, and communal benefit differ. Pre-2026 research on platform power and online rating bias highlights these tensions but lacks a structured analysis of competing ethical systems in technology management.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling and integrating multiple ethical frameworks (e.g., communitarian, virtue ethics, Ubuntu) into the design and governance of rural tourism platforms will reveal significant, actionable conflicts and synergies that a single-framework approach obscures, leading to more equitable and contextually appropriate technology deployment.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-agent simulation model where agents (representing tourists, locals, platform operators) operate under distinct ethical frameworks, formalized as sets of weighted constraints and utility functions. Drawing from Law's recent work, we will adapt the interference suppression and adaptation tracking algorithms from robotic guidance to manage conflicts between these ethical 'agents' in a dynamic system. The simulation will be parameterized with data from case studies on rural tourism platforms and the performance metrics of technologies like the Gyroscopic Wave Energy Converter (GyroWEC), analyzing factors between service provision, economic benefit, and cultural preservation under different ethical governance models.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, computational methodology for pluralistic ethical analysis in tourism technology studies. It will produce a typology of ethical conflict points in platform governance specific to rural contexts and offer design principles for adaptive systems that can negotiate between multiple value sets, moving beyond the 'one-size-fits-all' ethical assumption.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the formal computational integration of diverse philosophical ethical frameworks into a dynamic model of platform governance, and in the creative adaptation of control algorithms (from robotic fish guidance) to manage ethical interference, a method not previously applied to socio-technical system design in tourism.

proposals.title: Governing the Platformization of Rural Tourism: A Responsible Research and Innovation Framework for Technology Management
proposals.motivation: The rapid deployment of digital platforms in rural tourism, while promising for economic development, raises critical questions about power imbalances, data governance, and community impact. Pre-2026, the discourse on tourism tech management lacks a structured ethical framework to anticipate and mitigate these socio-technical risks. The EU's Responsible Research and Innovation (RRI) policy offers a mature, pre-2026 framework for addressing such issues, but its application to the specific context of rural tourism platforms remains unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying the RRI framework (with its dimensions of anticipation, reflexivity, inclusion, and responsiveness) to the management of rural tourism platforms will reveal systematic governance gaps and enable the design of more equitable and sustainable technology deployment strategies.
proposals.method: We will conduct a mixed-methods study. First, a quantitative analysis of online rating data from rural tourism platforms will adapt methods from consumer variety-seeking research to identify bias patterns linked to platform power. Second, we will employ a qualitative, multi-stakeholder case study in a rural community, using RRI as a scaffold to analyze interviews with tourists, hosts, platform operators, and local officials. The gyroscopic wave energy converter (GyroWEC) study's experimental-numerical approach for performance under complex conditions inspires our analytical model for evaluating platform 'performance' under conflicting stakeholder pressures.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce a novel, actionable RRI-based assessment tool for tourism technology managers and policymakers. It will contribute empirically to the study of power dynamics in platform economies and theoretically advance the application of RRI principles beyond its traditional STEM domains into service and tourism management.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the transdisciplinary fusion of the EU's RRI policy framework with empirical tourism platform studies, specifically targeting the under-examined rural context and integrating insights from seemingly disparate fields like wave energy converter performance analysis and online rating bias.

proposals.title: Algorithmic Impact Assessment for Rural Tourism Platforms: A Framework for Evaluating Power Dynamics and Service Equity
proposals.motivation: The rapid digitization of tourism, particularly in rural areas, increasingly relies on algorithmic platforms for service matching, pricing, and reputation management (e.g., online ratings). Pre-2026 research highlights how these systems can embed biases, exacerbate power imbalances between local providers and global platforms, and affect community welfare. However, structured, technical methodologies to proactively audit these impacts within tourism data ecosystems are lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying a formalized algorithmic impact assessment (AIA) framework to rural tourism platform architectures will reveal significant, previously hidden correlations between algorithmic factors (e.g., recommendation logic, rating aggregation) and systemic outcomes related to power distribution and service equity for local stakeholders.
proposals.method: The study will adapt AIA methodologies from pre-2026 AI ethics literature (e.g., risk assessment frameworks, fairness metrics) to a tourism context. We will conduct a numerical and experimental case study on a simulated or anonymized real-world rural accommodation platform. The method involves: 1) Decomposing the platform's key algorithms (matching, ranking, pricing) into modular 'factors'; 2) Applying sensitivity analysis and counterfactual testing to measure the 'interference' and 'jitter' (variance) in outcomes for different stakeholder groups under varying input conditions; 3) Using insights from wave energy converter gyroscopic stability analysis to model the system's response to destabilizing 'exciting motions' like demand shocks or biased data influx.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first technically-grounded framework for algorithmic auditing specific to tourism data systems, moving beyond qualitative ethics checklists. It will contribute a novel methodology for quantifying power dynamics in platform ecosystems and offer actionable insights for designing more equitable rural tourism services.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the cross-disciplinary translation of algorithmic assessment tools from AI ethics and stability analysis from engineering (gyroscopic systems) to the empirical study of tourism platforms, creating a new lens to analyze the 'hidden drivers' of systemic bias and power concentration.

ideas.title: Agent-based modeling of AI assistant adoption to overcome cultural costume barriers in tourism
ideas.description: This research uses agent-based modeling (ABM) to simulate the co-evolution of two diffusion processes within a tourist destination: the adoption of AI travel assistants and the social interactions inhibited by traditional cultural costume. The model agents (tourists and locals) have heterogeneous traits influencing both their likelihood of adopting an AI assistant and their sensitivity to costume as a social barrier. By simulating social networks and interaction rules, the research can identify tipping points and network pathways where AI assistant adoption most effectively reduces interaction inhibition, providing a computational framework to test proactive, technology-mediated strategies for cultural integration.

ideas.title: Segment-Specific Primacy of Experiential Value in AI Travel Adoption
ideas.description: This research investigates how the primacy of individual experiential value over social influence in driving initial AI adoption for travel differs systematically across traveler segments (e.g., solo, family, business). It posits that the weight given to personal utility versus social norms is not uniform but is instead moderated by the unique decision-making context and core needs of each segment. The study would employ a comparative, segment-focused methodology to identify which traveler types are true 'experiential pioneers,' adopting AI tools based on direct functional benefits despite low social proof, and which remain 'social conformists.' This reveals the conditional and segment-bound nature of social influence's role in the early adoption of AI for travel.

ideas.title: Quantifying the Ecosystem Integration Effect: A/B Testing AI as a Familiar Tool vs. Novel Stimulus in Travel
ideas.description: This research applies rigorous consumer research experimental designs (like A/B testing) to directly test the core proposition of Idea A. Instead of merely arguing that AI should be treated as a familiar tool, we integrate different AI interaction frames (novel stimulus vs. seamless tool) into existing travel tech platforms (e.g., booking engines, itinerary planners). We then measure causal effects on identity-related outcomes (e.g., perceived self-efficacy, traveler identity congruence) and practical metrics (adoption, trust, usage depth). This approach transforms a conceptual stance into a testable hypothesis within the complex ecosystem where adoption actually occurs.

ideas.title: Longitudinal analysis of parasocial bonds with AI cultural guides during mass festival immersion
ideas.description: This research tracks the longitudinal evolution of parasocial bonds between tourists and an AI-powered cultural guide (e.g., a Hanfu or heritage expert assistant) across multiple trips and within a single, extended festival visit. It specifically compares how these bonds intensify, stabilize, or fracture during the high-intensity, socially saturated experience of a mass cultural festival versus during routine, low-intensity daily attraction visits. The study examines if the AI's role shifts from an informational tool to a social companion or anxiety buffer during overwhelming festival crowds, and how repeated festival interactions alter long-term trust and relational expectations with the AI.

ideas.title: Disentangling and Modeling the Utilitarian-Parasocial Interaction in Algorithmic Travel Guidance
ideas.description: This research tests the hypothesis that perceived utility and parasocial connection are not simple competitors but interact to mediate acceptance of AI or influencer-based travel planning. A mixed-method design first uses a conjoint experiment to quantify the utility value of influencer features (e.g., demonstrated expertise, data-driven style) separate from likability. A subsequent longitudinal diary study then tracks users' real-world interactions with a travel planning agent, employing experience sampling to measure moment-to-moment shifts in perceived efficiency and relational feeling, allowing for analysis of their dynamic interplay and conditional dominance in driving sustained use.

ideas.title: A Co-Evolutionary Simulation Framework for AI Travel Assistants: Dynamically Calibrating Adoption Models with Panel Data and Embedded Experiments
ideas.description: This research creates a robust, empirically grounded simulation by coupling an agent-based model with a longitudinal panel study and an embedded field experiment. The panel tracks real user perceptions, usage, and social referrals over time, providing dynamic data to calibrate and validate the model's core parameters. Simultaneously, a randomized controlled experiment manipulates key predictors (e.g., trust signals, feature access) within a real platform, testing causal mechanisms and feeding results back into the simulation. This iterative, multi-method system allows the model to evolve beyond static assumptions, capturing the dynamic feedback between technology improvement, social diffusion, and behavioral change.

ideas.title: Longitudinal Study of Cultural Identity 'Decay' and Reinforcement Post-Immersive Experience
ideas.description: This research adapts the longitudinal methodology from immunology/vaccinology, where the durability of an immune response is tracked post-vaccination, to study the persistence of identity changes following an immersive cultural tourism experience. The analogous problem—understanding how an induced biological state changes over time and what reinforces it—is solved by measuring biomarker decay curves and testing booster doses. Adapted to the Hanfu tourism context, the study would longitudinally track tourists' self-reported and behaviorally measured identity markers (the 'cultural antibody titer') at scheduled intervals after their return home. It would quantitatively model the decay rate of the identity shift. Furthermore, the research would experimentally test low-touch 'cultural booster' interventions (e.g., targeted social media content, virtual meetups, or receiving a Hanfu accessory) delivered at predicted points of decay to see if they significantly prolong or reactivate the identity changes, thereby identifying mechanisms for sustaining transformative tourist experiences.

ideas.title: Scale variant: Regional AI models for local tourism

ideas.title: Leveraging the 'First-Hand Efficacy' Adoption Model from Medical Technology
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from medical technology adoption, where devices are often adopted based on immediate, private experiential value (e.g., a glucose monitor providing personal health data) rather than social influence. The analogous solution involved prioritizing first-hand efficacy and clear personal feedback. Adapted to AI travel adoption, the research would investigate contexts and design principles where the AI tool delivers a similarly immediate, tangible, and personally verifiable benefit—such as optimizing for a highly individualized constraint or preference—that allows its value to be assessed directly by the user, thereby inverting the typical primacy of social influence in driving initial trial and adoption.

ideas.title: Analyzing Hanfu Experience as a Social Contagion Process in Mass vs. Niche Settings
ideas.description: This research adapts epidemiological modeling to study the Hanfu experience. The analogous problem is understanding how disease spread differs between a short, dense gathering and a dispersed community. The solution uses compartmental models (like SIR) with context-dependent parameters. Adapted, the idea is to model Hanfu adoption as a social contagion process, comparing the rapid, peer-influenced 'transmission' and intense experience during a mass cultural festival against the slower, more deliberate engagement at daily attractions. By quantifying factors analogous to contact rate and susceptibility, the study will reveal how scale transforms the dynamics of cultural participation and identity performance.

ideas.title: AI as Cultural Mediator: How Generative AI Errors in Virtual Heritage Experiences Affect Identity Formation and Trust
ideas.description: This research investigates how errors or hallucinations by generative AI (like ChatGPT or image generators) within virtual, at-home cultural heritage experiences—such as AI-guided traditional costume try-ons or ritual simulations—impact user trust and cultural identity formation. It moves beyond studying trust in isolation to examine how AI inaccuracies (e.g., inventing or misrepresenting cultural elements) become active participants in the user's process of interpreting and constructing their own cultural identity. The study will explore whether these errors lead to rejection of the technology, uncritical acceptance of misinformation, or a more complex, reflective engagement with cultural authenticity. This synergy reveals AI not just as a tool for experience, but as an imperfect agent that co-constructs cultural understanding and trust dynamics in deeply personal contexts.

ideas.title: Examine role of social media in the process

ideas.title: Linguistic Analysis of Performative Discomfort in AI-Facilitated Cultural Encounters
ideas.description: This research applies conversational analysis from linguistics to examine dialogues in AI-guided cultural or heritage tourism scenarios, specifically where the activity involves performative elements like wearing traditional dress. It investigates how the AI's conversational structure and expertise either amplify or mitigate a participant's discomfort or weakened cultural connection. By quantifying linguistic features such as hedging, disfluencies, topic shifts, and coherence breakdowns in these chats, the study can model the relationship between guide discourse and tourist identity negotiation. This reveals how automated expertise can inadvertently trigger or soothe identity dissonance during culturally immersive acts.

ideas.title: Longitudinal study on parasocial interaction evolution

ideas.title: Negotiating Heritage in Real-Time: A Multi-Actor Study of Conflict and Accommodation in Historic Spaces
ideas.description: This research moves beyond tourist-centric observation to investigate the dynamic, often contested negotiations surrounding modern presence in traditional spaces. It simultaneously studies tourists, local residents, heritage site staff, and the digital narratives they produce to map the full spectrum of tensions and accommodations. The method combines in-situ interviews, participatory mapping with locals, and analysis of geo-tagged social media to understand how rules, perceptions, and performances of identity are co-constructed. The outcome is a robust model of heritage space as a living arena of social negotiation, not just a passive backdrop.

ideas.title: Agent-Based Modeling of Cross-Service AI Adoption Dynamics via Parasocial SOR Pathways
ideas.description: This research synthesizes agent-based modeling (ABM) with the extended Stimulus-Organism-Response (SOR) and parasocial interaction framework to simulate the co-evolutionary adoption of multiple, interconnected AI services within a population. Agents make adoption decisions for a portfolio of services (e.g., travel assistant, tour guide, concierge bot) based on personalized SOR pathways influenced by social network effects, parasocial bonds, and inter-service synergies or conflicts. The model allows researchers to test how design features, marketing strategies, and network structures for one service dynamically alter the diffusion landscape for entire AI tool ecosystems, predicting non-linear tipping points and strategic interventions for optimal ecosystem growth.

ideas.title: Apply conversational analysis from linguistics
ideas.description: Analyze dialogue structure and coherency in AI-tourist chats to quantify expertise and its link to acceptance.
ideas.sourceTitle: Tourist acceptance of ChatGPT in travel services: the mediating role of parasocial interaction

ideas.title: A Cross-Cultural Framework for Intangible Heritage Revitalization: Adapting a Conservation Biology Model
ideas.description: This research adapts the 'flagship species' model from conservation biology to the domain of intangible cultural heritage. The analogous solution involves using a charismatic species to generate support and resources for protecting an entire ecosystem. Adapted here, the Hanfu experience model is formalized as a 'flagship cultural artifact' framework, designed to measure how revitalizing a specific traditional costume stimulates engagement with its broader cultural context (crafts, rituals, values). This standardized framework is then applied to other cultural dress forms in different destinations, such as the kilt, kimono, or kente, to conduct comparative, cross-cultural analysis of heritage revitalization mechanisms and their systemic efficacy.

ideas.title: Apply physiological sensing from human-computer interaction

ideas.title: Analyzing power and production in the commercialization of traditional dress
ideas.description: This research critically investigates the political economy and power structures behind the modernization and commercialization of traditional costumes. It moves beyond user experience to analyze the entire chain of production, marketing, and consumption, explicitly examining tensions over cultural authority, economic benefit, and symbolic meaning. The methodology combines discourse analysis of branding and media with targeted ethnographic interviews of cultural custodians, artisans, and commercial designers. The goal is to produce a robust framework for understanding when and how commercialization supports cultural sustainability versus when it constitutes extractive appropriation.

ideas.title: Robot interactions are inferior to human social connection
ideas.description: Study if certain tourist segments, like anxious travelers, find robot interactions more comfortable and less stressful.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Theory to Map AI Service Parasocial Interaction Landscapes
ideas.description: This research seed proposes applying the SOR (Stimulus-Organism-Response) and parasocial interaction model to other AI services like virtual tour guides or hotel concierge bots. Drawing an analogy from Ecology, we adapt the solution of modeling species' ecological niches using Hutchinson's n-dimensional hypervolume. The analogous solution involves quantifying a species' position along key environmental gradients to predict its survival and competition. Adapted to this AI context, we treat AI services as species within a 'user interaction ecosystem.' We define the dimensions of a parasocial interaction niche—such as emotional valence, functional utility, temporal dynamics, and relational depth—to create a predictive framework. This allows researchers to map where a given AI tool (e.g., a tour guide vs. a therapeutic chatbot) sits in this landscape and systematically adapt the core SOR model parameters to fit its specific niche, enabling more effective and context-sensitive design of parasocial bonds across diverse AI applications.

ideas.title: Co-constructing a comparative framework for traditional dress engagement through collaborative ethnography
ideas.description: This research initiates a multi-sited, collaborative project where scholars partner with local cultural practitioners, historians, and communities in selected global destinations to first document and understand the specific socio-cultural dynamics surrounding their traditional dress. These parallel ethnographic studies focus on contemporary practices, meanings, and community-led revival efforts. The insights are then synthesized to build a robust, comparative analytical framework that identifies common themes, divergent challenges, and context-sensitive metrics for measuring cultural engagement, sustainability, and authenticity. The outcome is a co-created knowledge system that respects cultural specificity while enabling rigorous cross-cultural analysis of traditional dress in the modern world.

ideas.title: Use epidemiological contact tracing models to map costume-driven social interaction networks
ideas.description: This idea adapts the problem-solving framework from epidemiology, specifically contact tracing models used to map disease transmission networks. The analogous solution involves identifying an index case and reconstructing a temporal-spatial network of contacts based on proximity and interaction parameters. Adapted to the research context, the distinctive tourist costume is treated as the index case or diffusion source within a social, rather than pathogenic, network. By employing methods from digital contact tracing (e.g., anonymized Bluetooth proximity sensing), direct observation, and participant recall, researchers can map the chains of social interactions—such as questions, photo requests, or group formations—initiated by the costume wearer. This allows for the quantitative analysis of interaction networks, identifying key hubs (super-spreader locations), measuring the decay of engagement over time and distance, and modeling the flow of social attention and connection prompted by the costume within the tourist environment.

ideas.title: Contextually-Adaptive Validation of Parasocial AI Interaction Models Across Service Taxonomies
ideas.description: This research begins by developing a comprehensive taxonomy of AI service contexts (e.g., informational, transactional, experiential, advisory) based on user goals and relational parameters. For each identified service class, it then conducts a structured adaptation of the SOR and parasocial interaction model, tailoring core constructs and measurement instruments to the specific context. The system employs a multi-phase validation approach, using pilot studies within each service class to test the adapted model's efficacy before any broader comparative or generalizing research is undertaken, ensuring robust and meaningful findings.

ideas.title: Quantum Decoherence in Cultural Landscapes: Measuring Identity Signal Loss in Modern Attire at Heritage Sites
ideas.description: This research adapts the framework of quantum decoherence to study the identity negotiations of tourists wearing modern clothing in deeply historic settings. In the analogous field of Quantum Information Science, decoherence explains how a quantum system's coherent state deteriorates through environmental interaction. The adapted idea treats the tourist's modern attire and associated identity as a coherent 'signal' entering the powerful 'environmental field' of the traditional space. The study would develop methodological tools to measure the rate, intensity, and social triggers (e.g., gaze, rules, personal reflection) that cause this modern identity to 'decohere' or adapt, effectively scaling the interaction from the individual's internal negotiation (micro) to its observable social performance (macro).

ideas.title: Grounding Conversational Analysis: Validating AI Tourist Expertise via Fact-Checked Dialogue Simulation
ideas.description: This research develops a robust method to quantify AI expertise in tourist assistance by moving beyond surface-level dialogue coherency. It employs a simulated chat environment where AI responses to common and complex tourist queries are automatically fact-checked against curated, location-specific knowledge bases and rated for practical utility by human experts. The resulting validation dataset is used to critically evaluate and refine linguistic metrics of expertise, distinguishing genuinely informative dialogue from persuasive but unhelpful chatter. This approach directly links measured conversational quality to verified informational accuracy and actionable guidance.

ideas.title: Micro-ethics of frontline tech interactions
ideas.description: Scale down to study the moment-by-moment ethical dilemmas in tourist-staff-technology service encounters.
ideas.sourceTitle: Technology evaluation: a systematic review and implications for ethical technology management in tourism and hospitality

ideas.title: Trust-Calibrating AI: A Framework for Designing Skeptical User Pathways Across Service Contexts
ideas.description: This research synthesizes the low-trust starting point principle with the expansion to diverse AI service contexts (e.g., concierge bots, virtual guides) to develop a novel framework for 'trust-calibrating' AI design. It investigates how the Stimulus-Organism-Response (SOR) and parasocial interaction models must be specifically adapted or extended when initial user skepticism is the default, not an exception, across different service domains. The core synergy is generating evidence-based design protocols that define which interaction sequences, transparency cues, and reliability demonstrations are most effective for building trust in specific high-stakes or novel service contexts, where user assumptions and risk perceptions vary significantly. The outcome is a contingency model that maps service context characteristics to optimal trust-building pathways for initially skeptical users.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Niche Theory to Model Identity Construction in Hanfu Social Media Ecosystems
ideas.description: This research adapts the ecological concept of 'niche construction' to analyze Hanfu identity formation on social media. The analogous solution from ecology involves modeling how a species defines its role within an ecosystem through resource use and adaptation. Here, social media platforms are treated as distinct digital habitats. The adapted study will examine how individuals sharing Hanfu photos strategically curate their content and self-presentation to construct a sustainable identity 'niche,' optimizing for platform-specific resources (engagement) while navigating environmental pressures like algorithmic visibility and community expectations. This approach frames identity not just as internal expression, but as an adaptive performance within a competitive digital ecosystem.

ideas.title: Longitudinal Psychophysiological Mapping of AI Travel Assistant Bonding
ideas.description: This study conducts a longitudinal investigation where participants' repeated interactions with an AI travel assistant are tracked over multiple trips. Concurrently, psychophysiological measures (e.g., EEG for neural correlates of trust, eye-tracking for cognitive load and engagement) are collected during key, phase-specific interactions (e.g., planning, crisis management, post-trip review). This design uniquely allows researchers to model how the neurophysiological signature of the parasocial bond strengthens, decays, or recalibrates over time and context, moving beyond self-report to objectively identify the moments and patterns that solidify or erode the human-AI relationship.

ideas.title: Multi-Method Behavioral Tracking of Implicit and Explicit AI Assistant Bonding
ideas.description: This study investigates the evolution of human-AI bonds by moving beyond subjective surveys to a triangulated, behavioral methodology. It continuously logs fine-grained interaction patterns during travel planning and execution, while periodically administering implicit association tests and in-context choice experiments to capture non-conscious attachment. By correlating behavioral shifts, implicit biases, and self-reported perceptions over time and across trip phases, the design constructs a robust, multi-faceted model of how functional reliance transforms into parasocial connection.

ideas.title: Cultural Interface Mismatch: When Traditional Attire Creates Social Friction
ideas.description: This research investigates the dual problem where traditional costume, intended as a facilitator of cultural identity, instead acts as a barrier to social interaction at a destination. Drawing an analogy from Human-Computer Interaction (HCI), where a poorly designed user interface creates friction by mismatching user mental models, the study adapts the HCI solution of 'adaptive interfaces' or 'context-aware design.' The analogous HCI solution involves systems that detect user context and adjust UI elements to reduce cognitive load. Adapted to this context, the research will explore 'adaptive cultural presentation'—strategies where the presentation of traditional attire is dynamically modulated (e.g., through simplification, explanatory cues, or contextual partial alteration) in response to specific social interaction contexts. The aim is to develop and test practical frameworks that allow cultural expression to be maintained while minimizing unintended social friction, thereby improving the quality and depth of visitor-local interactions.

ideas.title: Biometric Mapping of Modern Identity in Historic Spaces
ideas.description: This study investigates the psychophysiological impact of wearing modern, everyday clothing within deeply historic tourist sites, using biometric sensors (e.g., EDA, HRV, EEG) to measure emotional arousal and cognitive load. It seeks to correlate physiological data with post-experience interviews about identity, belonging, and temporal perception. This approach reveals the unconscious stress or comfort of embodying the present in the past, providing an objective lens on how architectural historicity physically interacts with contemporary self-presentation. The research quantifies the often-intangible feeling of anachronism, offering new insights into embodied heritage experience.

ideas.title: Protocol-Driven A/B Testing of AI Interaction Styles on User Identity and Decision-Making
ideas.description: This research combines think-aloud protocols with controlled A/B testing to investigate how different AI interaction styles (e.g., formal vs. conversational, directive vs. collaborative) influence user identity construction and decision-making processes. Participants are assigned to interact with different AI system variants in a scenario like travel planning, while continuously verbalizing their thoughts and feelings. This allows researchers to quantitatively compare outcome measures (e.g., choice confidence, perceived self-efficacy) across conditions while simultaneously analyzing the qualitative, process-level data to explain the observed effects. The synergy provides a robust, multi-layered understanding of the psychological impact of AI design, moving beyond 'what works' to uncover 'how and why it works' on user identity.

ideas.title: Apply Clinical Consultation Discourse Analysis to AI-Tourist Chats
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from Medical Communication Research, specifically the Roter Interaction Analysis System (RIAS) used to code and quantify doctor-patient consultations. The analogous solution involves categorizing verbal exchanges (e.g., information-giving, partnership-building, emotion-handling) to objectively measure consultation quality and link it to patient outcomes like satisfaction and adherence. Adapted to the research context, this involves applying a modified RIAS framework to code transcripts of AI-tourist chats, quantifying the AI's 'expertise' as a multidimensional construct visible in dialogue structure (e.g., ratio of information-giving to responsive questioning, use of empathetic or rapport-building utterances). These quantified discourse metrics are then statistically correlated with user acceptance measures—such as trust, perceived usefulness, and intent to follow recommendations—collected via post-interaction surveys, thereby creating an empirical link between conversational coherence and user acceptance.

ideas.title: Applying the Proteus Effect to AI Embodiment: A Framework for Measuring Acceptance and Behavioral Change
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea by drawing an analogy from avatar research in Virtual Reality. The analogous problem is understanding how a user's representation (an avatar) influences their own psychology and behavior, known as the Proteus Effect. The analogous solution involves controlled experiments manipulating avatar traits and measuring outcomes. Adapted to this context, the study would challenge the assumption of text-only interaction by embodying ChatGPT with systematically varied vocal and visual characteristics. The adapted research idea is to use this framework to measure how different embodiments alter parasocial interaction dynamics, specifically testing for a 'Proteus Effect' where user acceptance, trust, and behavior are shaped by the AI's embodied form, moving beyond simple preference to measure consequential behavioral change.

ideas.title: Apply Clinical Psychology Process Research Methods to AI Interaction Analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from clinical psychology process research, specifically the Interpersonal Process Recall (IPR) method, to the context of studying human-AI interaction during travel planning. In IPR, a therapist and client review a session recording to retrospectively surface the client's internal processes. Adapted here, after a user interacts with an AI travel planner, the researcher conducts a recall session where the user watches a replay of their interaction. The user is instructed to pause the playback at any moment to verbalize their real-time thoughts, decision-making rationale, emotional responses, and trust judgments that were not explicitly stated during the original task. This method combines the richness of retrospective recall with the concrete anchor of the interaction log, capturing a more detailed and reflective protocol of the user's cognitive and emotional journey than concurrent think-aloud protocols alone, which can be intrusive and incomplete.

ideas.title: A Psychophysiological and Ecological Momentary Assessment of Hanfu Photo Curation and Sharing on Social Media
ideas.description: This study investigates the dynamic, micro-level process of Hanfu identity performance online by measuring real-time psychophysiological arousal and self-reported states during content creation and sharing. It employs a lab-based experiment where participants prepare and simulate posting Hanfu photos while their galvanic skin response and gaze patterns are recorded to index implicit self-consciousness. This is complemented by a two-week ecological momentary assessment, where participants use a smartphone app to report their feelings at key stages of actual social media posting. Finally, reflective interviews synthesize these data streams to build a robust model of how platform affordances and audience feedback loops shape identity construction.

ideas.title: Triangulated Assessment of Costume Experience via Physiology, Self-Report, and Behavior
ideas.description: This research system employs a multi-method framework to precisely characterize the emotional experience of wearing a costume. Biometric sensors (e.g., EDA, heart rate) continuously measure physiological arousal, while participants provide periodic self-reports using a standardized scale like the Self-Assessment Manikin (SAM) to indicate emotional valence and dominance. Concurrently, video recordings of participant behavior are coded for specific expressive cues. By synchronizing and analyzing these three data streams, the system can differentiate between, for example, the high arousal of delight and the high arousal of distress, providing a robust and nuanced understanding of the costume-wearing experience.
