ideas.title: Differential Impact of AI Error Typology and Context on the Dynamics of Trust and Behavioral Reliance
ideas.description: This research systematically investigates how specific categories of AI errors (factual inaccuracies, logical flaws, confident hallucinations, and social biases), combined with varying levels of error transparency and task criticality, differentially erode or calibrate user trust across its core dimensions. Using a controlled, factorial experiment, participants interact with a simulated AI assistant across different scenarios where pre-programmed error types are introduced. The study measures shifts in perceived competence, integrity, and benevolence, and then observes actual behavioral reliance on the AI in a follow-up, high-stakes decision-making task to move beyond self-reported acceptance.

ideas.title: Applying Phylogenetic Network Models to Trace the Evolution and Hybridization of Traditional Costume Forms
ideas.description: This research seed expands by challenging the assumption of authentic costume use through an analogy with evolutionary biology. In phylogenetics, the solution to the analogous problem of non-linear evolution (via horizontal gene transfer) was the development of phylogenetic network models. Adapting this solution, the research would examine modernized/commercialized traditional costumes not as corruptions, but as hybrid forms within a cultural phylogenetic network. The adapted idea involves creating such network models to trace the complex evolution and hybridization of costume elements, systematically mapping how traits from 'traditional' lineages interact with and are transformed by commercial, globalized, and modern design influences, providing a visual and analytical framework for understanding cultural transmission without the bias of seeking pure authenticity.

ideas.title: Biographical and Embodied Costume Analysis
ideas.description: This method constructs a performative biography of the costume by tracking its material, social, and symbolic life across time. It combines traditional object-based interviews with video-elicitation using rehearsal footage, first-person wearer accounts of physical interaction, and facilitated conversations between designers and performers. This triangulation shifts focus from the costume as a mere artifact to the costume as an active agent in a dynamic network of creation, embodiment, and interpretation. The resulting data captures the lived materiality of costume within the kinetic and collaborative processes of theatre.

ideas.title: Apply Material Culture Interview Protocols from Archaeology to Costume Analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts a methodological solution from archaeology and material culture studies to expand the original seed idea. In archaeology, 'object biography' interviews use artifacts as central prompts to collaboratively reconstruct their life histories, revealing layered social and technical narratives. By applying this protocol to costume studies, the research will conduct materiality-focused interviews that guide participants through a structured biographical exploration of a specific costume—from conception and construction to use, adaptation, and afterlife. This adapted method will systematically elicit richer, more nuanced data about the embodied knowledge, collaborative processes, and cultural meanings materialized in the costume, which are often overlooked in less structured object-based discussions.

ideas.title: Disentangling Modality from Parasocial Cues: A Factorial Study of AI Embodiment and Social Dynamics
ideas.description: This research systematically isolates the effects of sensory modality from parasocial relationship-building cues in human-AI interaction. It employs a controlled experiment where an AI assistant's outputs are presented across text, voice, and avatar modalities, while the density of parasocial cues within the conversation is independently varied. This design allows for precise measurement of how modality influences the acceptance of social cues, and which cue-modality combinations are most effective. The result is a granular, causal map of interaction dynamics that provides actionable design principles, moving beyond a simple comparison of text versus embodied interfaces.

ideas.title: Ecological Longitudinal Panel for Identity Practice Formation
ideas.description: This research moves beyond snapshot A/B tests by implementing a multi-phase, mixed-methods longitudinal panel. Participants are initially exposed to varied costume presentation styles in a controlled yet socially embedded setting. Their engagement is then tracked over 6-12 months using integrated data streams: periodic psychometric scales on identity salience and belonging, digital diary entries documenting real-world wear and social interactions, and culminating in semi-structured interviews. This design captures not just immediate preference but how presentation acts as a catalyst for identity work, examining the consolidation, abandonment, or transformation of identity practices through lived experience and social feedback.

ideas.title: Comparative Micro-dynamics of Situated Self-Perception Across Identity-Triggering Contexts
ideas.description: This research maps the minute-by-minute dynamics of self-perception change as it unfolds across multiple, common real-world activities that serve as recognized triggers for identity shift. Instead of a single ritual, the system uses experience sampling and physiological/behavioral sensors to capture data during diverse, pre-identified 'trigger contexts' such as professional dressing, role-specific digital interface use, and pre-performance routines. By comparing the temporal signatures of self-reported and sensor-derived states across these contexts, the design distinguishes universal micro-processes of self-concept transition from the idiosyncratic effects of any one activity. This yields a robust, generalizable model of how the self is reconstituted in real time during everyday transitions.

ideas.description: This research develops and validates quantitative scales to measure self-identity expression and relational attachment outcomes specifically within interactions with embodied AI agents (e.g., voice-based or avatar-based ChatGPT). It moves beyond studying mere acceptance to rigorously test how different interaction modalities (text-only vs. voice vs. avatar) alter the depth and nature of parasocial bonds and identity exploration. This allows for direct comparison of how embodiment features influence psychological outcomes, providing an empirical foundation for designing AI that responsibly manages user attachment and identity integration.

ideas.title: Adapting Vaccine Hesitancy Models to Build Trust in AI from a Low-Trust Baseline
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea by drawing an analogy from public health's approach to vaccine hesitancy. In that field, trust is not assumed; practitioners model acceptance pathways for skeptical individuals by mapping a confidence continuum and deploying interventions tailored to specific drivers of distrust. Adapting this, the research would model AI acceptance pathways from a deliberate low-trust starting point. It posits a 'reliance readiness continuum' and identifies analogous, mutable cognitive and social drivers of AI distrust (e.g., opacity, control alienation, misaligned incentives). The adapted solution is to design and test 'trust inoculation' protocols. These are structured, incremental interaction sequences where skeptical users engage with AI in low-stakes, high-transparency scenarios with clear user veto power, facilitated initially by a trusted human or institutional intermediary. The goal is to generate a validated framework for moving users along the reliance continuum by systematically addressing identified distrust drivers, mirroring evidence-based public health interventions.

ideas.title: High-Temporal-Resolution Psychophysiological Tracking of Self-Concept Transition
ideas.description: This research adapts the high-temporal-resolution tracking approach from systems neuroscience to the psychological study of self-perception. The analogous problem—understanding how a neural circuit transitions between states during a behavior—is solved by using millisecond-precise electrophysiology synchronized to task events. Here, the 'circuit' is the individual's self-concept system, and the 'behavioral task' is the initial costume dressing ritual. The adapted solution employs a multi-modal micro-interaction analysis: video coding of gestures and pauses, continuous psychophysiology (EDA, HR), and ultra-brief, repeated self-report scales administered via a smart device at minute-by-minute intervals throughout the dressing sequence. This method allows for the creation of a second-by-second 'phase map' of self-perception transition, identifying critical inflection points (e.g., a shift in agency or identity valence) tied to specific ritual actions like fastening a closure or putting on a signature accessory, thereby solving the problem of capturing the dynamic microstructure of identity construction.

ideas.title: Adapting Human-Robot Interaction Biofeedback Protocols for AI Parasocial Interaction
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from Human-Robot Interaction (HRI), where real-time psychophysiological biofeedback is used to make collaborative robots adaptive and trustworthy. The analogous HRI solution involves monitoring a human's cognitive and emotional state via EEG and GSR to adjust robot behavior. Adapted to AI parasocial interaction, the research would implement a closed-loop system where an AI agent's dialogue and expressive delivery are modulated in real-time based on instantaneous EEG-derived emotional metrics and eye-tracking-derived pupillary responses. This creates a dynamic, physiologically-informed interaction aimed at fostering connection, with the adaptation protocols themselves serving as a novel, objective measure of parasocial bond formation.

ideas.title: Apply Clinical Trial Blinding Protocols to Costume Presentation A/B Tests
ideas.description: This idea adapts the rigorous blinding methodologies of clinical trial design to the original concept of A/B testing costume presentation styles. In clinical pharmacology, double/triple-blind protocols are the gold standard for isolating a treatment's effect from bias. The analogous solution involves blinding participants, intervention administrators, and outcome assessors to group assignment. Adapted to this context, the research would implement a multi-blind A/B test where the costume presentation style (e.g., virtual try-on vs. static image) is the 'treatment.' Participants are randomly assigned, but the style is delivered through a standardized interface that conceals the condition from the participant, the researcher interacting with them, and the coder evaluating subsequent identity outcome measures. This controlled approach would provide a more definitive causal assessment of how presentation style alone influences identity outcomes.
